steps.title: Identify the structure around the blank
steps.content: The sentence reads: "claims ______ Valerie Trouet, can tell the history of the world." The blank falls between the verb "claims" and the full name "Valerie Trouet." The word before the name is a professional title (dendrochronologist) that identifies who is making the claim. In standard English, a title used directly before a proper name functions like an appositive descriptor and takes no punctuation between the title and the name.

steps.title: Eliminate each wrong choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'dendrochronologist:': a colon after the title incorrectly separates the descriptor from the name it modifies. ✗
  • Choice B — 'dendrochronologist,': a comma between the professional title and the proper name it introduces is not standard usage. ✗
  • Choice C — 'dendrochronologist—': an em dash here creates an abrupt break that severs the title from the name it directly precedes. ✗
  • Choice D — 'dendrochronologist': no punctuation between the title and "Valerie Trouet" keeps the descriptor and name as a single unit, exactly as standard English requires. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
